title: Cognitive Profile

_validation_notes: These pairwise sums are enforced in application logic, not expressible in pure JSON Schema.

expressive_vagueness_baseline.description: Self-reported answer to whether the user generally finds it hard to put technical thinking into words. Used by ticket-coach to calibrate how readily an ambiguous-sounding response is treated as a comprehension signal versus just this person's normal communication style — true raises the bar before something is flagged as ambiguous, so concise or imprecise phrasing isn't repeatedly mistaken for not understanding.

response_language_variant.description: Maps a short language code to a regional variant code only (e.g. 'en': 'en-GB'). This field is NOT for vocabulary-complexity instructions or free-text style notes — that calibration is handled solely by is_english_native. Values must be short locale-style codes, not prose.

known_technologies.description: Programming languages, frameworks, libraries and tools the user has worked with. Deliberately broader than just programming languages, so that ticket-coach can build analogies from any prior technology, not only language-to-language ones (e.g. 'this is like Redux, but for server state' is just as valid an anchor as a language comparison).

type.description: What kind of technology this is. 'language' = programming language (e.g. PHP, Go). 'framework' = e.g. Laravel, React. 'library' = e.g. Redux, Zustand. 'tool' = e.g. Docker, a CI/CD system. 'platform' = e.g. AWS, a cloud or infra platform.

proficiency_confidence.description: Accumulated evidence score toward the NEXT proficiency upgrade, maintained by cognitive-profile's FLOW D. Rises by +0.15 per scorecard with strong signal for this technology, falls by -0.10 per scorecard with weak signal, clamped to [0, 1]. At >= 0.70 the upgrade fires and this resets to 0. This is derived state, not something the user sets directly.

struggling_concepts.description: Specific programming concepts the user has had genuine difficulty with, surfaced either through end-of-ticket reflection (ticket-coach §7) or inferred from a real blocker during a session (e.g. needing real code rather than pseudocode on the same concept repeatedly). Used to bring relevant concepts back deliberately in future tickets that touch them, rather than treating every ticket as isolated. This is NOT the same as cognitive_weights (how the user prefers to learn) — this is what topics, specifically.

concept.description: A fixed, language-agnostic taxonomy of concepts, deliberately closed so the same struggle doesn't get recorded under several different names across tickets (e.g. always 'loops', never 'bucles' or 'for-loops' as separate entries).

scope.description: Either the literal string 'general' (the concept is difficult across languages/contexts, e.g. testing discipline), or a technology name matching an entry in known_technologies[].name (the concept is specifically difficult in that technology, e.g. concurrency in Go but not in PHP).

confidence.description: 'self-reported': the user said so directly in end-of-ticket reflection. 'observed': inferred from behaviour (e.g. repeated real-code escalations on the same concept) without the user naming it themselves.

last_reinforced_date.description: The last date ticket-coach deliberately brought this concept back for reinforcement in a relevant ticket. Absent if it has never been reinforced yet.

resolved.description: Set true once the concept has been deliberately reinforced and gone well on a subsequent occasion (ticket-coach's judgement, not an automatic count). A resolved entry is kept for history rather than deleted, but ticket-coach stops actively bringing it back once true.

seniority_level_confidence.description: Accumulated evidence score toward the NEXT seniority_level upgrade, maintained by cognitive-profile's FLOW D. Rises by +0.15 per scorecard with strong overall signal, falls by -0.10 per scorecard with weak signal, clamped to [0, 1]. At >= 0.70 the upgrade fires and this resets to 0. Separate from any single technology's proficiency_confidence — see FLOW D for how the two interact. This is derived state, not something the user sets directly.

field.description: Descriptive dot-path of the non-axis field that changed, for human/LLM readability only — not a literal JSON Pointer. 'known_technologies' is an array of objects (matched by their 'name' field, not by object key), so a path like 'known_technologies.go.proficiency' means "the proficiency field of the known_technologies entry whose name is Go", not a literal nested object access. E.g. 'known_technologies.go.proficiency' or 'background_context.seniority_level'.

evidence_summary.description: Brief note on what scorecard evidence justified this change, e.g. '4/5 recent Go tickets resolved without code help'.

label.description: Describes the axis itself (theory vs concrete-example preference), never a cognitive_weights key name such as 'code_examples_first'.
Describes the axis itself (system-overview vs incremental-build-up preference), never a cognitive_weights key name.
Describes the axis itself (narrative-text vs diagram preference), never a cognitive_weights key name such as 'diagrams'.
Describes the axis itself (guided-steps vs exploratory preference), never a cognitive_weights key name such as 'guided_steps'.
Describes the axis itself (technical-precision vs real-world-analogy preference), never a cognitive_weights key name such as 'real_world_analogies'.

_business_rules_not_expressible_in_json_: delta_max per reassessment = 0.5 (score cannot move more than this in a single update_history entry)
Minimum evidence threshold: at least 3 of last 5 sessions must show consistent drift signal before automatic_drift_threshold trigger fires
agreement-to-score formula: new_score = clamp(previous_score + (user_agreement - 3) * 0.3, previous_score - 0.5, previous_score + 0.5), then clamp to [1, 5] overall
weight formula per pair: weight_B = (score - 1) / 4; weight_A = 1 - weight_B
